text.title: Axum handler pattern: extractors, DTOs, response shapes
text.summary: Defines the canonical Axum handler structure: extractor ordering (State first, body last), request DTOs with validator derives, response DTOs with From<Model> impls, and consistent return types.

rule.statement: Order Axum extractors: State first, path/query parameters next, body (Json<T>) last. Body extractors consume the request; placing them before path/query compiles but body extractors after another body extractor will not compile.
rule.rationale: Axum processes extractors in declaration order. Json<T> consumes the request body; a second body extractor after it finds an empty stream and panics or returns an error.

antipattern.description: Placing Json<T> before State or Path parameters.
antipattern.reason: Does not fail at compile time (just extractor ordering) but produces confusing behavior when State is added later and causes trait bound errors.

rule.statement: Define separate request DTOs (with Deserialize + Validate derives) and response DTOs (with Serialize). Implement From<Model> for response DTOs. Never expose domain models directly in handlers.
rule.rationale: Decoupling request shape from the domain model lets both evolve independently. From<Model> implementations are compile-checked and avoid ad-hoc field mapping in handlers.

rule.statement: AppState must implement Clone. Wrap all non-Clone resources in Arc. Never put &str, non-Send types, or borrowed data in AppState.
rule.rationale: Axum clones AppState for each request. Non-Clone types cause compile errors; non-Send types violate the async executor's thread-safety requirement.

antipattern.description: Putting a raw DbPool or Config struct directly in AppState without Arc.
antipattern.reason: Forces a full clone of potentially large structs per request. Use Arc<DbPool> and Arc<Config> instead.
